---
title: "BIOSTAT 724 Final Project (Working Progress)"
author: "Patrick Bautista & Izzi"
date: '`r Sys.Date()`'
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```


```{r}
# Bayesian Regression for Used-Car Price Distribution
# Data Loading, Cleaning & Descriptive Statistics

#Libraries & data
library(tidyverse)   # loads dplyr, ggplot2, readr, etc.
library(tibble)
library(knitr)
library(janitor)
library(corrplot)

cars <- read_csv("../data/train.csv")
# Initial look at the data
head(cars)
dim(cars)
str(cars)
summary(cars)
```

```{r}
# Cleaning 

# Run clean_names() FIRST so column names have no spaces
cars <- cars %>%
  clean_names()
# Now convert columns to correct types using clean names
cars <- cars %>%
  mutate(
    brand        = as.factor(brand),
    model        = as.factor(model),
    fuel_type    = as.factor(fuel_type),
    transmission = as.factor(transmission),
    accident     = as.factor(accident),
    clean_title  = as.factor(clean_title)
  )
# Check missing values
colSums(is.na(cars))
# View selected columns sorted by brand and price
cars_view <- cars %>%
  select(brand, model, model_year, milage, fuel_type, clean_title, price, accident) %>%
  arrange(brand, desc(price))

head(cars_view)

# First 3 rows per brand
cars_view %>%
  group_by(brand) %>%
  slice_head(n = 3) %>%
  ungroup()

# Remove 817 exact duplicate rows before any analysis
cars <- cars %>%
  distinct(brand, model, model_year, milage, fuel_type, clean_title, price, accident,
           .keep_all = TRUE)

cat("Rows after removing duplicates:", nrow(cars), "\n")
```


```{r}
# Filter to top 12 brands 
# Analysis restricted to 12 most frequently observed brands (n >= 1300 each),
# ensuring stable estimation while preserving diversity in vehicle characteristics.

top_brands <- c("BMW", "Ford", "Mercedes-Benz", "Chevrolet", "Audi", "Porsche",
                "Toyota", "Lexus", "Jeep", "Land", "Cadillac", "Nissan")
# Land Rover is referred to as "Land" 

cars_top <- cars %>%
  filter(brand %in% top_brands)

cars_top %>%
  count(brand, sort = TRUE)
```

```{r}
# Brand counts for plotting
brand_counts <- cars_top %>%
  count(brand, sort = TRUE)

# Horizontal bar chart: vehicle count by brand
ggplot(brand_counts, aes(x = reorder(brand, n), y = n)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#DB7093") +
  coord_flip() +
  geom_text(aes(label = scales::comma(n)), hjust = -0.1, size = 4) +
  labs(
    title = "Vehicle Count by Brand",
    x = "Brand",
    y = "Number of Vehicles"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    panel.grid.major.y = element_blank()
  ) +
  expand_limits(y = max(brand_counts$n) * 1.1)
```

```{r}
#  Log-transform price
# Raw prices are right-skewed with high variance. Log-transforming stabilises
# variance and better satisfies normality assumptions for the regression model.

cars_top <- cars_top %>%
  mutate(log_price = log(price))

# Side-by-side plot proving the log transformation reduces skewness
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
hist(cars_top$price,     breaks = 40, main = "Raw price",             xlab = "Price ($)")
hist(cars_top$log_price, breaks = 40, main = "Log-transformed price", xlab = "log(Price)")
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

```{r}
# Overall summary statistics

# Full-sample log-price summary
cars_top %>%
  summarise(
    n        = n(),
    mean     = mean(log_price),
    median   = median(log_price),
    sd       = sd(log_price),
    skewness = (mean(log_price) - median(log_price)) / sd(log_price),
    min      = min(log_price),
    max      = max(log_price)
  )

# Summary stats by brand
cars_top %>%
  group_by(brand) %>%
  summarise(
    n          = n(),
    mean_log   = mean(log_price, na.rm = TRUE),
    median_log = median(log_price, na.rm = TRUE),
    sd_log     = sd(log_price, na.rm = TRUE),
    var_log    = var(log_price, na.rm = TRUE),
    q1_log     = quantile(log_price, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    q3_log     = quantile(log_price, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(mean_log))
```

```{r}
# Continuous predictor summary (mileage and model year)
# Note: mileage SD (~50,000) vs model_year SD (~5.6) — very different scales.
# This motivates standardising predictors before Gibbs sampling so priors
# N(0, 100^2) are equally weakly informative for all coefficients.
cars_top %>%
  summarise(
    mileage_min  = min(milage),
    mileage_mean = mean(milage),
    mileage_sd   = sd(milage),
    mileage_max  = max(milage),
    year_min     = min(model_year),
    year_mean    = mean(model_year),
    year_sd      = sd(model_year),
    year_max     = max(model_year)
  )

# Outlier check on raw price
# Extreme prices will widen the posterior predictive distribution (Aim 3).
ggplot(cars_top, aes(y = price)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  labs(title = "Raw price — outlier check", y = "Price ($)")

cars_top %>%
  summarise(
    n_extreme   = sum(price > 200000),
    pct_extreme = round(100 * mean(price > 200000), 2)
  )
```


```{r}
# Categorical predictor frequency tables
# Low-n groups (e.g. Plug-in Hybrid, Diesel) will produce wider posterior
# credible intervals — flagged here to anticipate Aim 2 results.

cars_top %>%
  count(accident) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1))

cars_top %>%
  count(transmission) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1))

cars_top %>%
  filter(!fuel_type %in% c("–", "not supported")) %>%
  count(fuel_type, sort = TRUE) %>%
  mutate(pct = round(100 * n / sum(n), 1))
```

```{r}
# Plots — all predictors vs log-price

# Log-price by brand (sorted by median)
ggplot(cars_top, aes(x = reorder(brand, log_price, median), y = log_price)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Log-price by brand", x = "Brand", y = "log(Price)")

#  Log-price distribution faceted by brand
ggplot(cars_top, aes(x = log_price)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30) +
  facet_wrap(~ brand, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of log-price by brand", x = "log(Price)", y = "Count")

#  Mileage vs log-price — supports linear assumption (Model Assumption 2)
ggplot(cars_top, aes(x = milage, y = log_price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Mileage vs Log-Price", x = "Mileage", y = "log(Price)")

#  Model year vs log-price — supports linear assumption (Model Assumption 2)
ggplot(cars_top, aes(x = model_year, y = log_price)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Model Year vs Log-Price", x = "Model Year", y = "log(Price)")

#  Accident history vs log-price
cars_top %>%
  mutate(accident_label = ifelse(
    grepl("None", accident), "None Reported", "Accident Reported"
  )) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = accident_label, y = log_price, fill = accident_label)) +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Accident History vs Log-Price", x = "", y = "log(Price)")

#  Fuel type vs log-price
cars_top %>%
  filter(!fuel_type %in% c("–", "not supported")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = fuel_type, y = log_price, fill = fuel_type)) +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Fuel Type vs Log-Price", x = "Fuel Type", y = "log(Price)")

#  Transmission vs log-price
cars_top %>%
  mutate(trans_label = ifelse(
    grepl("A/T|Automatic|CVT|Dual Shift", transmission, ignore.case = TRUE),
    "Automatic", "Manual"
  )) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = trans_label, y = log_price, fill = trans_label)) +
  geom_boxplot(show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(title = "Transmission vs Log-Price", x = "", y = "log(Price)")

#  Correlation matrix of numeric predictors
# Checks for multicollinearity — high correlations inflate posterior uncertainty.
num_vars <- cars_top %>%
  mutate(
    accident     = ifelse(grepl("None", accident), 0, 1),
    transmission = ifelse(grepl("A/T|Automatic|CVT|Dual Shift",
                                transmission, ignore.case = TRUE), 1, 0)
  ) %>%
  select(log_price, milage, model_year, accident, transmission) %>%
  cor(use = "complete.obs")

corrplot(num_vars, method = "color", type = "upper",
         addCoef.col = "black", tl.col = "black",
         title = "Correlation Matrix of Numeric Predictors",
         mar = c(0, 0, 1, 0))
```
